title: Using the Converse to Test for a Right Triangle

steps.instruction: If a² + b² = c² (where c is the longest side), then the triangle is a right triangle.
steps.work: Test: does a² + b² equal c²?
steps.explanation: The converse lets us check if any triangle is right, given three sides.

steps.instruction: The longest side is 17. Check if 8² + 15² = 17².
steps.explanation: Since a² + b² = c², this IS a right triangle.

steps.instruction: The longest side is 9. Check if 5² + 7² = 9².
steps.explanation: Since a² + b² ≠ c², this is NOT a right triangle.

title: Classifying Triangles as Acute, Right, or Obtuse

steps.instruction: Compare a² + b² to c² (c = longest side).
steps.work: a² + b² = c² → right. a² + b² > c² → acute. a² + b² < c² → obtuse.
steps.explanation: This extends the Pythagorean converse to all triangle types.

steps.instruction: c = 7. Calculate a² + b² and compare to c².
steps.explanation: Since the sum of squares of the shorter sides exceeds c², all angles are less than 90°.
The angle opposite the longest side is greater than 90°.
